Triggers.value: People who are related to the event

Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.

Negative Appraisals.value: Nothing good can happen to me anymore
I have to be especially careful because you never know what can happen next
I can't trust that I will do the right thing

Maladaptive Strategies.value: Being reminded of the event by others

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: Direct contradiction within Memory node: 'Verbal Access' (easy verbal recall) conflicts with 'Snapshot Memory' (fragmented, pictorial recall) and 'Bodily Reenactment' (somatic re-experiencing), which typically co-occur with impaired narrative coherence. This combination is clinically implausible for the same trauma memory.

issues.item: Snapshot Memory: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
issues.explanation: Direct contradiction within Memory node: 'Snapshot Memory' (fragmented, pictorial recall) conflicts with 'Verbal Access' (easy verbal recall). These represent opposing memory states that cannot plausibly coexist for the same traumatic memory.

Clinically implausible with trauma type: Police trauma does not typically generate exercise avoidance as a maladaptive coping strategy. This item lacks thematic connection to the trauma context and appears inconsistent with other avoidance strategies focused on event-related triggers.

issues.item: Bodily Reenactment: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
issues.explanation: Bodily reenactment is inconsistent with photographic/visual recall items in the same node. Bodily reenactment suggests somatic re-experiencing and dissociation, while photographic recall suggests clear, intact visual memory without somatic intrusion - these represent opposing memory phenomenologies that rarely coexist in the same patient.

Avoiding police as a trigger is inconsistent with the absence of police-related triggers in the Triggers component. The triggers listed (moral violation, stress, event-related people) don't include police, yet police avoidance is a primary maladaptive strategy, creating a cross-node inconsistency in the trauma context.

issues.item: Risky Behavior: Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
issues.explanation: Risky behavior contradicts the constant vigilance and carefulness expressed in Negative Appraisals ('I have to be especially careful'). These represent opposing behavioral tendencies - hypervigilant caution versus risk-taking - that are clinically implausible to coexist as primary symptoms.

issues.item: Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
issues.explanation: Directly contradicts 'Memory Detachment: I detach myself from the memories' from Maladaptive Strategies. Photographic recall implies vivid, intrusive memory access, while detachment suggests ability to emotionally distance from memories - these opposing states are clinically implausible to coexist.

issues.item: Memory Replay: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
issues.explanation: Directly contradicts 'Memory Detachment: I detach myself from the memories' from Maladaptive Strategies. Intrusive film-like replay is incompatible with successful detachment from memories.

issues.item: Memory Detachment: I detach myself from the memories
issues.explanation: Directly contradicts all three Memory items (Photographic Recall, Visual Memories, Memory Replay) which describe vivid, intrusive, replaying memories. Successful detachment would prevent such vivid intrusive recall.

vignette:
Mrs. A, a 62-year-old widowed woman of Middle Eastern descent, presents with a profound sense of life being irrevocably shattered. Her world fractured following a traumatic encounter with police, the details of which remain locked in a form she cannot articulate. She reports that nothing good can happen to her anymore and that she must maintain constant vigilance, as she can no longer trust herself to do the right thing.

Her daily life is dominated by intrusive, sensory-laden memories. When she sees people who remind her of those involved in the event, or witnesses what she perceives as moral violations in others, she is often flooded with vivid, frightening images and the sounds from that day. These intense, nonverbal memories make it impossible for her to concentrate. They also trigger sudden, overwhelming flashbacks where she feels as if the event is happening again, plunging her into a state of hyper-alertness and fear.

This pervasive sense of threat, fueled by these unbidden memories and her core beliefs about a dangerous future and her own incompetence, dictates her behavior. She actively avoids the entire area where the event occurred, any conversations that might remind her of it, and all contact with police. The need to manage the overwhelming distress from her flashbacks and hypervigilance further compels these avoidant actions.

Consequently, Mrs. A’s world has shrunk to a narrow, isolated routine. The cognitive effort required to stay on guard and the disruptive power of her sensory memories leave her exhausted and detached. Her life is organized around preventing the resurgence of those terrifying images and sounds, which in turn reinforces her belief that the world is perpetually unsafe and that she is fundamentally incapable within it.
